coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and its derivatives for multifarious photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Zhiping
Chen, Shufen
Tan, Timothy Thatt Yang
Xiao, Fang-Xing
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a unique category of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, has ignited tremendous interest in the past few years owing to its gorgeous electronic and physicochemical properties in conjunction with quantum-confinement and edge effects. In this review article, we predominantly focus on the recent developments of GQDs-semiconductor composited systems for heterogeneous photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis under ambient conditions including non-selective pollutant degradation, selective organic transformation, hydrogen or oxygen production, CO2 reduction, and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. It is anticipated that our current review article will provide enriched information on the promising applications of GQDs and its derivatives and simultaneously, offer new insights for exploring a large variety of GQDs-semiconductor composited systems for advanced solar energy conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photoelectrocatalysis
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GQDs-based nanomaterials for photo-induced multifarious catalysis
Photocatalytic non-selective degradation of organic pollutants

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The award program for Minjiang Professorship from Fuzhou University is greatly acknowledged. The financial support from the project of Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) ( 21703038 ) is acknowledged. This research was supported by the Nanyang Technological University Start-up Grant ( M4081326 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic prosess toward the fabrication of photoluminescent GQDs by utilizing unsubstituted HBC as a carbon precursor. Reproduced with permission [58]. Copyright 2011 American Chemical Society.
Flowchart for synthesis of GQDs using small molecules. Reproduced with permission [60].Copyright 2010 American Chemical Society.

(a & b) Mechanism for hydrothermal deoxidization of GR NS into GQDs, and (c) three electronic transitions of triple carbenes at zigzag sites. Reproduced with permission [25]. Copyright 2010 Wiley Publishing Group.

(a) Schematic illustration of oxidation-induced carbon fiber cutting into GQDs, (b) TEM, (c) AFM image, (d) height and size histogram and (e) HRTEM image of the synthesized GQDs. (f & g) Schematic illustration of the edge of zigzag and armchair directions. Reproduced with permission [73]. Copyright 2012 American Chemical Society.

(a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of GQDs and GOQDs by chemical exfoliation of graphite nanoparitcles. (b) TEM, (c) high-resolution (HR) TEM, (d) AFM image, and (e) height profile and height distribution of GQDs. (f) TEM image, (g) HRTEM, (h) AFM, and (i) height profile and distribution of GOQDs. Reproduced with permission [74]. Copyright 2013 Wiley Publishing Group.

Schematic illustration depicting the synthetic process toward the preparation of GQDs using the diamond-edge-induced nanoscale-cutting of graphite. (a) Graphite nanoblocks were produced from HOPG block by using the diamond knife. (c) Two-step nanotomy process to produce graphite nanoblocks, (b) flowchart for fabricating GR nanoribbons and GQDs from graphite nanoblocks with chlorosulphonic acid and (d) SEM images of GQDs with different shapes. Reproduced with permission [79]. Copyright 2012 Nature Publishing Group.

(a) Schematic illustration of PEC water splitting mechanism of GQDs/ZnO NWs photoelectrodes. Reproduced with permission [81]. Copyright 2013 Wiley-VCH. (b) Schematic illustration of the photoexcited charge carriers transfer process at the GQDs/TiO2 interfaces. Reproduced with permission [82]. Copyright 2016 Elsevier Publishing Group. PEC hydrogen evolution performances of ZnO NWs, GQDs/ZnO NWs and N-GQDs/ZnO NWs under (c) simulated solar light irradiation and (d) visible light irradiation. (e) Schematic illustration of the PEC water splitting mechanism of N-GQDs/ZnO NW heterostructure. Reproduced with permission [84]. Copyright 2016 Royal Chemical Society.

(a) Effect of applied potential on the visible-light-driven PEC reduction of Cr(VI) over Fe2O3-GQDs/NF-TiO2 nanocomposite with corresponding rate constants and estimated electrochemical enhancement E% displayed in (b). (c) Scheme depicting the PEC reduction of Cr(VI) Fe2O3-GQDs/NF-TiO2 nanocomposite with corresponding photogenerated charge carriers transfer process. Reproduced with permission [87]. Copyright 2017 Elsevier Publishing Group.

(a) HRTEM image of TiO2/GQDs nanocompsite, (b) photocatalytic H2 production performances of pristine TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs nanocomposites, and (c) comparison on the photocatalytic H2 production performances of different samples. (e) Long-time H2 production performance of TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite for cyclic runs. (d & f) Schematic illustration of photocatalytic H2 prodution mechanism for TiO2/GQDs, in which GQDs act as (d) photosensitizers and (f) electon acceptors & reaction center. Reproduced with permission [89]. Copyright 2017 Elsevier Publishing Group.

(a) Configuration and energy diagram of NGO-QD photochemical diode, (b) photocatalytic H2 production performances of NGO-QD under visible light irradiation. Reproduced with permission [49]. Copyright 2014 Wiley-VCH. (c) Schematic illustration of photocatalytic mechanism for interpreting enhanced photocatlytic activity of NGQDs-ZnNb2O6/g-C3N4 nanocomposite. Reproduced with permission [92]. Copyright 2017 Royal Society of Chemistry.

(a) HRTEM image and (b) photocatalytic performances of L-ZnO/S N-GQDs nanocomposites toward the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. (c) Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic mechanism of L-ZnO/S,N-GQDs heterstructures. Reproduced with permission [102]. Copyright 2017 Elsevier Publishing Group. (d) HRTEM image and (e) photocatalytic performances of Ag-GQDs-ZnO ternary nanocomposites toward RhB degradation under visible light irradation. (f) Scheme depicting the photogenerated charge carriers transfer mechanism of Ag-GQDs-ZnO ternary nanocomposite. Reproduced with permission [103]. Copyright 2015 Royal Chemical Society.

(a) HRTEM image of GQDs@SnO2 heterostructures and (b) schematic illustration of the photocatalytic mechanism of GQDs@SnO2 nanocomposites. Reproduced with permission [104]. Copyright 2016 Elsevier Publishing Group. (c) TEM image of GQDs/BiVO4 heterostructure and (d) schematic illustration of the visible-light-driven charge carriers transfer mechanism over GQDs/BiVO4 heterostructure. Reproduced with permission [106]. Copyright 2017 Elsevier Publishing Group.

(a) TEM image and (b) visible-light-driven photocatalytic performances of GQDs/mpg-C3N4 nanocomposites. (c) Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic mechanism of GQDs/mpg-C3N4 nanocomposite. Reproduced with permission [108]. Copyright 2017 Elsevier Publishing Group. (d) SEM image and (e) photocatalytic performances of GQDs-BM nanocomposites under visible light irradiation. (f) Schematic illustration of the photocatalysis mechanism of GQDs-BM nanocomposites. Reproduced with permission [109]. Copyright 2016 Royal Chemical Society.
GQDs and its derivatives for PEC and photocatalytic appliations.

row.entry: Using chitosan as the linker molecule
Zigzag edge effect, favorable HOMO-LUMO band alignment and band gap
Enhanced light absorption, Favorable energy levels
Synergistic interaction of nitrogen doping & photosensitization effect
Prolonged lifetime of photoexcited charge carriers
Photosensitizers and electron acceptors, high charge separation efficiency
Improved light harvesting capability and electron capturing efficiency
GQDs reacted with ANP and trieth amine
Boosted light-harvesting and electron transfer efficiency
Favorable bandgap, maximize visible-light harvesting ability
TiO2 films were immersed into GQDs solution
Favorable bandgap, maximize visible-light harvesting ability
GQDs was drop casted on TiO2
Enhanced light absorption, improved charge separation, promoted the formation of active oxygen and hydroxyl radicals
Enhanced light absorption, promoted the formation of active oxygen and hydroxyl radicals
GQDs and Cu NPs co-decorated ZnO [101]
Effective charge separation, high specific surface area, enhanced visible light harvest
Extended light absorption, high separation efficiency
Favorable bandgap, promoted the formation of active radicals
Graphite C3N4 decorated with S, N co-doped GQDs [107] GQDs/mpg-C3N4 [108]
Enhanced charge separation efficiency, promoted active species formation
Enhanced electrons-holes separation efficiency, in-situ production of highly active species

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and its derivatives for multifarious photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The recent developments of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and its derivatives for heterogeneous photocatalysis and photoelectrochemical water splitting were comprehensively and systematically summarized in conjunction with outlooks and future perspectives in these two booming research fields.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a unique category of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, has ignited tremendous interest in the past few years owing to its gorgeous electronic and physicochemical properties in conjunction with quantum-confinement and edge effects. In this review article, we predominantly focus on the recent developments of GQDs-semiconductor composited systems for heterogeneous photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis under ambient conditions including non-selective pollutant degradation, selective organic transformation, hydrogen or oxygen production, CO2 reduction, and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. It is anticipated that our current review article will provide enriched information on the promising applications of GQDs and its derivatives and simultaneously, offer new insights for exploring a large variety of GQDs-semiconductor composited systems for advanced solar energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, population explosion in tandem with severe environmental issues and upcoming energy crisis has become global tremendous challenges and it is an urgent obligation to develop novel, efficient and multifunctional materials to overcome these challenges. As one of the most common and essential elements in inorganic and organic materials, carbon plays a significant role in academic and industrial society. All of the carbon allotropes, including football-shaped buckminsterfullerene C60 discovered in 1985 [1], one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in 1991 [2], and two-dimensional (2D) graphene (GR) in 2004 [3] have become super stars in the history of nowadays materials developments. Especially, in the past few years, GR consisting of a single or few layer(s) of carbon atoms in a honeycomb lattice has attracted particular attention owing to its extraordinary thermal, mechanical and electronic properties [4,5].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a unique category of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, consisting of analogous framework to GR, has ignited tremendous interest in the past few years owing to its remarkable electronic and physicochemical properties associated with quantum-confinement and edge effects [6–22]. Compared with GR, GQDs is featured by ultra-small particle size (<10 nm) which affords substantially more oxygen-containing functional groups on the planar surface that can serve as highly active reaction sites. Alternatively, GQDs exhibit excellent solubility, chemical stability, low cytotoxicity and high luminesce, thus providing unprecedented opportunities for potential applications in a myriad of research fields [23–32]. In particular, high conductivity and large specific surface area of GQDs in conjunction with the various active sites on the planar surface render it a desirable and multifarious platform to integrate with diverse nanostructured materials for energy conversion and storage [33,34], such as photovoltaic cells [24,33–35], rechargeable batteries [36,37] and supercapacitors [38,39], as well as for environmental remediation [40], such as organic pollutant degradation,[41,42] bactericidal,[43,44] and water treatment [45–47]. Moreover, Femi level and band gap of GQDs can be modulated by particle size [48] and chemical potential of GQDs can also be sensitively tuned by heteroatom dopants, chemical groups, defects and edge configurations [49,50]. Benefited from these astonishing achievements over the past few years, the progress being made on developing sustainable GQDs-based systems has been profoundly accelerated.

Although some review articles on the bio-imaging and optoelectronic applications of GR and GQDs have been published [51–57], developments of GQDs and its derivatives for solar energy conversion in recent years have not yet been summarized. In this review article, we predominantly focus on the recent developments of GQDs-semiconductor composited systems for heterogeneous photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis including environmental remediation (e.g. pollutant degradation), photocatalytic hydrogen or oxygen production, CO2 photoreduction, and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. More specifically, we first enumerated the main two synthetic routes including bottom up and top-down approaches to fabricate the GQDs. In the following part, multifarious photocatalytic and PEC applications of GQDs and its derivatives-based composited systems were comprehensively and systematically elucidated. Eventually, future perspectives of GQDs for solar energy conversion were discussed. It is anticipated that our current review article will provide enriched information on the promising applications of GQDs and its derivatives in the field of solar energy conversion and, simultaneously, offer new insights for exploring a large variety of GQDs-semiconductor composited systems.

In general, synthetic routes toward the fabrication of GQDs can be divided into two broad groups: bottom-up and top-down approaches. Bottom-up approaches normally involve organic synthesis using small molecules with aromatic structures such as polycyclic aromatic compounds, which produces GQDs with excellent control over size distribution and tailored properties. Top-down approaches involve decomposing and exfoliating cheap graphite-based materials under harsh experimental conditions using concentrated acids with strong oxidizing capability at high temperature, but the size distribution and morphology of GQDs cannot be controlled precisely. These two types of synthetic strategies will be specifically discussed in the following sections.

GQDs can be assembled with small organic molecules or other precursors with comparable structure to the honeycomb carbon. As shown in Fig. 1 , Liu’s group fabricated mono-dispersed disk-like GQDs by using unsubstituted hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC) as the precursor which is subjected to the process of carbonization, oxidization, surface functionalization, and reduction [58]. The resultant GQDs show uniform distribution with an average size of 60 nm and demonstrate a high photoluminescence (PL) efficiency of 3.8%. This work judiciously harnesses the spontaneous self-assembly property of small molecules for progressive construction of GQDs.

Li’s group reported the preparation of colloidal GQDs via a step-wise organic route [59]. More specifically, 168, 132, and 137 conjugated carbon groups were first synthesized by oxidizing polyphenylene dendritic molecules and then GQDs consisting of these conjugated carbon groups and possessing tunable size were attained by stabilization of 2′, 4′, 6′-trialky phenyl molecule. Large colloidal GQDs with uniform size were further synthesized using the trialkyl phenyl groups to attach the QDs, which prevents stacking of GR nanostructure in 3D mode (Fig. 2 ) [60]. In the following work, this group utilized small benzene derivatives to produce nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and they found that increasing the nitrogen atoms with a specific bonding configuration can remarkably enhance the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) performances [61].

As carried out by Lu’s group, GQDs can also be prepared by a ruthenium-catalyzed caged-opening of C60 molecules [62]. To be specific, strong interaction between C60 molecules and catalyst induces the formation of vacancies on the ruthenium crystal surface, which produces carbon clusters from the fragmentation of the embedded C60 molecules. Subsequently, diffusion and aggregation of the carbon clusters form small GQDs, by which shape and size of GQDs can be finely tuned by annealing temperature.

In a similar work, Wang’s group synthesized a high-quality single-crystalline colloidal GQDs with small pyrene molecules by a hydrothermal method [63]. The resultant GQDs exhibit superior optical properties, such as obvious excitation absorption bands in the whole visible spectrum region, efficient excitonic fluorescence, long-term photostability and large molar extinction coefficients.

Heteroatom-doped GQDs can also be prepared by bottom-up methods. For example, Lin’s group developed a pyrolysis approach to fabricate N-GQDs with citric acid and amino compound [64]. The as-synthesized GQDs show a high quantum yield of 59.2% and is sensitive in the detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP) molecules. In an analogous way, Li’s group prepared sulfur-doped GQDs using fructose and sulfuric acid as the precursors [65]. Apart from the single atom-doped GQDs, S, N co-doped GQDs have also been fabricated by utilizing citric acid as the carbon precursor and thiourea as the sulfur and nitrogen source [66].

Aside from the bottom-up methods mentioned above, GQDs can also be prepared by cutting 2D GR nanosheets (NS) with chemical, electrochemical, or physiochemical means. Most of these top-down approaches differ in the ways they convert GR NS to QDs, which will be elucidated in the following part.

A hydrothermal approach has been developed to cut GR NS into GQDs with blue photoluminescence (PL) [25]. Two main steps are involved in this method, that is, chemical reduction of graphene oxide (GO) to GR NS and then oxidation of GR NS to GQDs in a concentrated acid under ultrasonication. Similar to the oxidation of CNTs to shorten the CNTs [67], an unzipping mechanism was proposed for this hydrothermal cutting method (Fig. 3 ). In this method, epoxy groups on the planar surface of GR NS tend to become a line on a carbon lattice under acid oxidation, thus leading to a rupture of the CC bonds. The resultant epoxy chain was further oxidized to epoxy pairs which converted to carbonyl pairs. Thereafter, the group was utilized to prepare the ultra-small and well-crystallized GQDs (ca. 3.6 nm) with high-temperature chemical cutting treatment [68]. The thus-prepared GQDs show strong and stable green PL and can be used as a fluorescent nanoprobe for cell imaging.

A facile method concerning hydrazine hydrate reduction of GO was developed to fabricate GQDs with surface passivation of PEG diamine [69]. The GQDs exhibit bright blue PL under an excitation of 365 nm laser and up-converted emission frequency because of the anti-stokes PL. In the following work, the same group developed a one-pot hydrothermal reaction to prepare GQDs-PEG, which possesses excellent PL quantum yield and up-conversion performances, as well as larger photocurrent density as compared with pristine GQDs [70]. Similarly, it was found that GQDs fabricated by a two-step chemical cutting process from GO and functionalized with amine groups exhibited PL shifts and band gap tuning [71], which was proved by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. In another typical example, Zhu and co-workers fabricated GQDs with controllable degree of surface oxidation based on a two-step approach with GO as the precursor and DMF as the solvent [30,72]. Significantly, GQDs with different degrees of oxidation display tunable PL behaviors.

Notably, GQDs can be prepared in large scale amount via a one-step process using carbon fibers as the precursor combined with an acid etching and exfoliation treatment, as demonstrated by Peng’s group (Fig. 4 ) [73]. In this way, large-scale preparation of GQDs with size ranging from 1 to 4 nm was achieved. By changing the reaction temperature, PL and bandgap of GQDs can be finely controlled. The authors also ascertained that the GQDs exhibited low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and being an eco-friendly material for bio-imaging. This work provides a feasible route to fabricate GQDs with mass production possibility, which is of great significance for potential applications of GQDs in the near future.

Besides the GO and carbon fibers that can be utilized as the precursors, graphite nanoparticles with mean size of around 4 nm can be chemically exfoliated to homogeneous graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) and GQDs with a large amount scale (Fig. 5 ) [74]. By rationally comparing the optical properties of GOQDs and GQDs, the authors demonstrated that GOQDs with oxygen-containing functional groups exhibit green luminescence and GQDs with pure sp2 carbon crystalline structure display blue luminescence and a fast recombination lifetime.

Alternatively, frequently observed carbon black was used to prepare single-layer and multi-layer GQDs with a large yield in a concentrated nitric acid solution, as reported by Li’s group [23]. It was revealed that single-layer and multi-layer GQDs can be separated by centrifugation in an acid solution. On the other hand, note that multi-layer GQDs exhibit substantial absorption in the UV–vis spectrum region, while single-layer GQDs display good PL performance and biocompatibility.

Qu et al. developed an electrochemical approach to break down GR film into functional GQDs with an average size of 3–5 nm [75]. Significantly, the thus-synthesized GQDs displayed a green luminescence and retain stable in aqueous solution for several months. Moreover, it was revealed that photovoltaic devices constructed by GQDs as electron acceptor show an impressive solar conversion efficiency of 1.28%. In a similar work, Yang’s group demonstrated the preparation of water-soluble, uniform-sized GQDs by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite combined with a hydrazine reduction [76], by which GQDs with strong yellow luminescence and good biocompatibility suitable for stem cell imaging were obtained.

An efficient microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach was developed by Lau’s group to prepare water-soluble GQDs using glucose as a precursor [27]. The emission energy of the GQDs can reach up to 4.1 eV, which has been determined as the largest emission energy among all the reported solution-based QDs. In addition, it was unraveled that diameter of the GQDs can be finely tuned from 1.65 to 21 nm by controlling the heating time.

A simple ultrasonic method was created by Lee’s group to fabricate GQDs with excitation-independent up-conversion and down-conversion PL [32]. The GQDs can be used as photosensitizer for rutile and anatase TiO2 toward the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants under visible light irradiatation.

Besides the approaches mentioned above, several other top-down methods have been developed in the past few years to fabricate high-quality GQDs. For instance, multi-walled CNTs and graphite flakes were exfoliated and disintegrated into GQDs with lithium/potassium intercalation [77], by which GQDs (20 nm) with great luminescence quantum yield and lower oxidation extent were obtained. In another case, GQDs can be precisely prepared by a high-resolution electron beam lithography method to cut GR NS [16]. This approach requires a high-precision device but only a small yield of GQDs was produced. Interestingly, self-assembled block copolymer (BCP) was judiciously utilized by Jeon’s group as an etching mask to fabricate size −controlled and uniform GQDs from chemical vapor deposition (CVD) grown GR [78], which produces GQDs with a hexagonal arrangement along with uniform morphology and size. Alternatively, as shown in Fig. 6 , Mohanty’s group demonstrated the preparation of GQDs with different predetermined shapes such as triangle, rectangle, square, ribbon and tailored dimensions by diamond-edge-induced nanotomy (nanoscale-cutting) of graphite into graphite nanoblocks and followed by exfoliation [79].

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs-based nanomaterials for photo-induced multifarious catalysis

ce:section.ce:para: Rational integration of GQDs with semiconductors provides an effective strategy to extend the promising potential applications of GQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites in the field of PEC water splitting. Some important works on GQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites for PEC and photocatalytic applications were listed in Table 1 . In specific, CdSe/GQDs heterostructures have been fabricated and utilized as a photoanode which showed remarkably enhanced photocurrent density and improved PEC hydrogen production performances under visible light irradiation as compared with CdSe and CdSe/GR counterparts [80], due primarily to the unique zigzag edge effect, favorable HOMO-LUMO band alignment and band gap of GQDs.

Apart from the narrow-band-gap semiconductors, GQDs has been utilized to sensitize the wide-band-gap semiconductors for enhancing the light absorption of semiconductors in the visible spectrum region. As demonstrated by Li’s group [81], GQDs have been uniformly deposited on the ZnO nanowire arrays (NWs) framework via an effective interfacial self-assembly approach. It was found that GQDs functionalized ZnO NWs (GQDs/ZnO NWs) heterostructures as photoanode exhibit significantly enhanced PEC water splitting performances under visible light irradiation in comparison with pristine ZnO NWs, which is predominantly due to the remarkably enhanced light absorption of ZnO in the visible spectrum region and the favorable energy levels between ZnO and GQDs (Fig. 7 a). More significantly, PEC water splitting performances of the GQDs/ZnO NWs heterostructures can be finely tuned by the deposition percentage of GQDs. This work first demonstrated the great potential applications of GQDs in boosting the PEC water splitting performances of wide-band-gap semiconductors for advanced solar energy conversion.

Besides ZnO, GQDs has also been directly assembled on the TiO2 hollow nanowires for PEC water splitting under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm), for which the pivotal dual roles of GQDs as photosensitizer and photocatalyst were determined, thereby leading to substantial enhancement of the charge accumulation and transfer in the interfacial region of nanocomposites during the PEC water splitting process (Fig. 7b) [82]. In another work, it was revealed that 3D micropillar (MP) or microcave (MC) arrays of rutile TiO2 nanorods (NRs) decorated with GQDs exhibit highly enhanced PEC water splitting performances compared with pristine TiO2 counterparts under the same conditions, which can be attributed to the fast charge separation between GQDs and rutile TiO2 [83]. Under simulated solar light irradiation, a large photocurrent density of 2.92 mA cm−2 was achieved on the MP-TiO2 NRs-GQDs nanocomposite with a very high incident-photon-to-current-conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 72% at 370 nm.

N-GQDs can also be utilized as an efficacious photosensitizer for highly efficient PEC water splitting and the cooperative synergy between nitrogen doping and GQDs was fully harnessed. For a typical example, N-GQDs/ZnO NWs heterostructures were fabricated by a facile and green electrostatic layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly method by utilizing positively charged polyelectrolyte and negatively charged N-GQDs as the assembly units, which gives rise to well-defined photoanode [84]. PEC investigations showed that N-GQDs/ZnO NWs heterostructure demonstrated remarkably enhanced PEC hydrogen production performances in comparison with GQDs/ZnO NWs and pristine ZnO NWs counterparts under both simulated and visible light irradiation (Fig. 7c), which is attributed to the synergistic interaction of nitrogen doping and photosensitization effect of GQDs in conjunction with their intimate interfacial contact [78]. This work provides a typical paradigm to judiciously harness the intrinsic surface charge properties of GQDs and element-doped GQDs for rational design of well-defined GQDs/semiconductor composited photoelectrodes for solar-powered energy conversion.

Apart from PEC water splitting devices for solar energy conversion, GQDs-based nanomaterials in some conditions have been used as efficient biosensors for PEC monitoring of targets and biosensing [85]. Alternatively, N-GQDs coupled with MoS2 NS has been used in ultrasensitive PEC biosensing for acetamiprid assay [86], the principle of which is based on the prolonged lifetime of photoexcited charge carriers on millisecond time scale and remarkably enhanced charge separation, thereby giving rise to substantial photocurrent signal of N-GQDs-MoS2 nanomaterials.

Besides, GQDs have also been explored for PEC reduction of heavy metal ions at ambient conditions. For instance, Fe2O3-GQDs/NF-TiO2 composite film was prepared by loading α-Fe2O3 and GQDs onto pyramid-shaped N and F co-doped TiO2 (NF-TiO2) [87], which showed far better PEC performances for Cr(VI) reduction and satisfactory catalytic stability when it was utilized as an anode (Fig. 8 ). The result is attributed to the synergistic effect of visible-light-active components (α-Fe2O3 and NF-TiO2) and extraordinary roles of GQDs as electron accelerator and photosensitizer. On the other hand, GQDs can also be photoexcited and the photoelectrons can transfer to the CB of semiconductor (i.e., Fe2O3 & TiO2).

Hydrogen as a source of clean energy has attracted tremendous attention during the past few decades for sustainable developments of human society. In particular, photocatalytic hydrogen production with the aid of solar light features a facile and green approach to achieve mass production of hydrogen in the future. Recent years have witnessed a cornucopia toward the fabrication of GQDs-based nanomaterials for photocatalytic hydrogen production. For example, Yu and co-workers reported that (100) faceted anatase TiO2 decorated with GQDs shows 8 times higher enhancement in hydrogen production rate compared with pristine (100) faceted TiO2 [88], which is ascribed to the crucial role of GQDs as electron trap, therefore giving rise to a higher charge separation efficiency and facilitating the photoexcited electrons transfer from GQDs to the conductive band of TiO2. Similarly, Min et al. showed that GQDs-coupled TiO2 nanoparticles (GQDs/TiO2 NPs) demonstrated highly active photocatalytic activity towards H2 production, which results in 7 and 3 times higher enhancement in H2 production efficiency and photocurrent density than TiO2 NPs alone under simulated solar light irradiation, respectively(Fig. 9 b,c) [89]. It has been ascertained that GQDs can serve as effective photosensitizers to maximize the light harvest (Fig. 9d) and simultaneously, as electron acceptors to enhance the charge separation to boost hydrogen production (Fig. 9f). The photogenerated electrons can easily transfer from the CB of GQDs (−3.55 to −3.09 eV vs.Vac) to the CB of TiO2 (−4.2 eV vs.Vac), hence resulting in efficient electron-hole separation and thus enhanced hydrogen evolution. Photocatalytic hydrogen production has also been observed over GQDs-modified TiO2 nanotube arrays (GQDs/TiO2 NAs) with GQDs uniformly loaded on the TiO2 NAs substrate, which demonstrated remarkably enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production performances under visible light irradiation due to weak light-filtering effect and improved charge separation and transfer efficiency afforded by GQDs [90]. Photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of GQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites is influenced by the loading amount of GQDs due to the possible formation of GQDs aggregates and thus-caused shielding effects. Moreover, good photocatalytic stability of GQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites can be retained after stand long-term runs.

Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (NGO-QDs) fabricated by GO NS can catalyze overall water splitting under visible light illumination. The unique configuration of NGO-QDs resulted in the formation of p-n type photochemical diodes (Fig. 10 a and b), in which the p- and n-domains were responsible for the H2 and O2 production, respectively [49]. Moreover, it was found that NGO-QDs deposited with Pt as co-catalyst showed high activity for H2 generation with 12.8% of quantum yield under visible light irradiation (λ = 420 nm) [50].

Noteworthily, a novel metal-free N-GQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst was reported and it showed remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity for H2 production under visible light irradiation relative to pristine g-C3N4 owing to the multiple roles of N-GQDs for improved light harvesting capability and electron capturing efficiency [91]. In a similar ternary system, NGQDs-ZnNb2O6/g-C3N4 heterostructures were rationally constructed and they demonstrated high hydrogen production rate of 340.9 μmol h−1 g−1 under visible light irradiation surpassing the corresponding counterparts under the same conditions. This can be ascribed to the cooperative synergy of components and unique heterojunction structure at the interface, which is in favor of generating more electron-hole pairs and accelerating the interfacial charge separation (Fig. 10c) [92].

Photocatalytic CO2 reduction to produce solar fuels is one of the hottest topics in solar energy research. Harnessing CO2 reduction technique would decrease the greenhouse effect and thus generate sustainable energy resources. Yadav and co-workers synthesized functionalized GQDs (fGQDs) as visible-light-driven photocatalysts for CO2 conversion to solar fuel (formic acid) [93]. For the first time, visible-light-driven GQDs-based photo- and bio-catalystic system was thus developed and utilized as a highly efficient photocatalyst for fuel production from CO2 reduction. Moreover, it was found that fGQDs showed significantly enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance toward the 1,4-reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (1,4-NADH) production and formic acid (HCOOH) formation as compared with the conventional counterparts, attributable to the boosted light-harvesting and electron transfer efficiency with minimum charge recombination on the fGQDs.

Coupling metal nanocrystals with GQDs is able to take full advantage of the synergistic interaction from the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of plasmonic metals and electron trapping effect of GQDs for target selective organic transformation. For example, Ag@GQDs nanocomposites showed remarkable photothermal effect owing to the SPR-excited local heating of Ag NPs, which is beneficial for electrons transfer in the reaction system, thereby promoting selective reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) [94].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic non-selective degradation of organic pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic removal of organic pollutants utilizing GQDs-based nanomaterials is of great significance for environmental remediation. For example, GQDs-sensitized TiO2 NAs heterostructures fabricated by a controllable electrophoretic approach showed high visible-light-driven photocatalytic performances toward methyl blue (MB) degradation with long-term stability. Moreover, it was unveiled that photocatalytic performances of GQDs/TiO2 NAs are superior to those of CdS and CdSe QDs-functionaliezed TiO2 NAs counterparts under the same conditions [95]. In another typical work, Gupta et al. synthesized GQDs-infilled TiO2 NAs heterostructure, which showed highly efficient photocatalytic activity toward MB degradation under UV light irradiation. This is mainly ascribed to the substantially enhanced light absorption in the UV region, improved separation of photogenerated charge carriers by expediting electrons transfer from the CB of TiO2 to the LUMO energy level of GQDs, and strong adsorption capacity of GQDs to MB molecules owing to π–π interaction [96]. Alternatively, it was found that photocatalytic performances of GQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites can be finely tuned by the parameter of GQDs. For example, as for the GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, the larger size range of GQDs, the narrower bandgap, and the more red-shifted absorption edge of the nanocomposites to the visible region, thus substantially improving the electron-hole separation toward the photo-degradation of organic dye pollutants (MB) under visible light illumination [97]. On the other hand, note that photocatalytic performance of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites can be boosted by replacing GQDs with element-doped GQDs. For example, S, N co-doped GQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts demonstrated excellent photocatalytic activity in degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation with degradation rate 3 times higher than that of N-GQDs/TiO2 [98]. Similarly, N, S co-doped GQDs-GR-TiO2 NAs ternary nanocomposites fabricated by Tian et al. [99] exhibited remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performances toward methyl orange (MO) degradation under visible light irradiation relative to single and binary counterparts.

Aside from TiO2, GQDs has also been intimately integrated with a large variety of other semiconductors for photocatalytic removal of organic pollutants. For instance, GQD/ZnO NWs heterostructures fabricated by Ebrahimi et al. displayed a considerable enhancement in photocatalytic degradation of MB under simulated solar light irradiation with an elegant direct Z-scheme mechanism [100]. Interesting, photoelectrons transfer occurred from the CB of ZnO (−4.2 eV vs. Vac) to the HOMO of GQDs (−4.28 eV vs. Vac), which is beneficial for the formation of steady electrons in the LUMO of GQDs and holes in the VB of ZnO for superoxide (O2
− ) and hydroxyl radical (OH−) production. It was unraveled that the optimal GQDs (0.4%)/ZnO NWs heterostructure demonstrated 3-fold enhancement in photoactivity as compared to pristine ZnO NWs. GQDs and Cu NPs co-decorated ZnO nanocomposites fabricated by a facile spin-coating method combined with an annealing treatment displayed remarkable improved photocatalytic performances toward RhB degradation under UV light irradiation compared with GQDs/ZnO and Cu/ZnO counterparts, which is attributed to the synergistic interaction of Cu NPs and GQDs [101]. In an another example, leaf-templated ZnO NPs integrated with S, N-codoped GQDs (L-ZnO/S, N-GQDs) nanocomposites showed high photocatalytic performances toward degradation of RhB under visible light illumination, owing to the integrative reasons including highly effective charge separation, high specific surface area, and enhanced visible light harvest endowed by GQDs functionalization (Fig. 11 a–c) [102]. A ternary photocatalyst composed of Ag NPs, GQDs, and ZnO was constructed by Wan’s group and the thus-prepared Ag-GQDs-ZnO heterostructure demonstrated excellent visible-light-responsive photocatalytic performances toward the degradation of RhB [103], which far outperforms the pristine ZnO and binary counterparts (i.e., GQDs-ZnO and Ag-ZnO) (Fig. 11d–e). This work highlights the synergistic cooperation of Ag NPs and GQDs in contributing to the promising photocatalytic performances of Ag-GQDs-ZnO ternary heterostructure.

GQDs@SnO2 heterostructures prepared by pyrolyzation of citric acid (CA) showed significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity toward MB degradation under visible light irradiation in comparison with pristine SnO2 nanorods (Fig. 12 a and b) [104], which is attributed to the extended light absorption in the visible spectrum region and high separation efficiency of photoexcited charge-carriers with uniform deposition of GQDs on the SnO2 substrate. The photogenerated holes in SnO2 can oxidize the adsorbed MB to CO2 and H2O. Simultaneously, the photogenerated electrons trapped by GQDs capture O2 to produce superoxide radicals (O2
•− ), which can further selectively oxidize the pollutant. Noteworthily, GQDs/AgVO3 heterojunctions fabricated by Lei et al. showed considerable enhancement in photocatalytic degradation of pharmaceutical pollutantsuch as Ibuprofen (IBP) under visible light irradiation in comparison with pristine AgVO3 nanoribbons, indicating GQDs exerts pronounced influence on boosting the photocatalytic performances of AgVO3. In addition, it was remarkable to find that GQDs plays a pivotal role in improving the stability of AgVO3 [105].

Tang et al. fabricated GQD/BiVO4 nanoribbons heterostructures, which showed significantly enhanced photocatalytic performances toward carbamazepine (CBZ) degradation under visible light irradiation compared with single counterparts [106]. Photocatalytic mechanism was further probed by the authors and the predominant in-situ formed active species during the photocatalytic degradation process was determined to be hydroxyl radicals (OH) (Fig. 12c and d). In this case, photoelectrons from the CB of BiVO4 nanoribbons (−3.17 eV) can transfer to the LUMO of GQDs (−3.27 eV) when the LUMO energy level of GQDs is lower than the CB bottom of semiconductors. The holes left on BiVO4 surface can oxidize water to OH for pollutants decomposition

Graphite C3N4 decorated with S, N co-doped GQDs prepared by a simple physical method exhibited significantly improved photocatalytic activity toward RhB degradation in comparison with GQDs/C3N4 and pristine C3N4 counterparts under visible light irradiation, which is ascribed to the efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes at the interface [107]. GQDs modified mesoporous g-C3N4 (GQDs/mpg-C3N4) nanocomposites have also been fabricated by a facile approach based on the electrostatic interactions. It was revealed that GQDs/mpg-C3N4 nanocomposites showed high photocatalytic activity toward RhB degradation and removal of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) pollutants under visible light irradiation. The promising photocatalytic performances of GQDs/mpg-C3N4 nanocomposites can be attributed to the in-situ production of a large amount of superoxide radical (O2 −) active species which give rises to the complete mineralization of organic pollutants (Fig. 13 a–c) [108].

Significantly, Hao et al. demonstrated that mesoporous Bi2MoO6 modified with sub–3 nm GQDs (GQDs-BM) (Fig. 13d–f) displayed promising broad-spectrum photo-degradation activities under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation, owing primarily to the remarkably enhanced electrons-holes separation efficiency and effective in-situ production of highly active species in the reaction systems such as OH [109].

In this review article, photo-induced multifarious catalysis including PEC water splitting and heterogeneous photocatalysis of GQDs-based nanomaterials have been comprehensively summarized. GQDs combine the unique optical and structural merits of QDs with extraordinary electrical properties of sp2 carbon nanomaterials, large specific surface area and abundant functional groups on the edges, thus rendering it a state-of-the-art nanomaterial as compared with conventional semiconductor QDs or other carbon-based nanomaterials such as CNTs and GR. Throughout this review article, a large variety of GQDs/semiconductor systems with unique nano-architectures and multifunctional properties have been comprehensively and systematically discussed. Particularly, PEC water splitting and diverse photocatalytic applications of GQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites including selective organic transformation, hydrogen production, CO2 reduction, and non-selective degradation of organic pollutants have been elucidated.

Many research progress on the developments of GQDs-based nanomaterials have been made in the past few years; however, further endeavors on these composited functional nanomaterials are highly expected to be integrated widely into industry to achieve breakthrough in the near future. Moreover, many challenges need to be fully overcome, such as expensive synthesis cost, extreme operating environment, poor system stability, and low efficiency. The perspectives for future research directions of GQDs are suggested as follows:
1.
The morphologies, sizes, structures, and properties of GQDs cannot be precisely controlled by a variety of fabrication methods as reported in previous works. Notably, bottom-up approaches such as dehydration or assembly of small molecules normally produce GQDs with large size distribution, which are not favorable for photocatalytic applications. On the other hand, top-down routes such as electrochemical etching can be applied to fabricate size-controlled GQDs from GR or CNTs; however, low yield and harsh reaction conditions of these top-down routes hinder the future potential applications of GQDs. Hence, developing efficacious synthetic strategies with large scale and low cost toward the fabrication of GQDs with narrow size distribution and highly-enhanced photocatalytic performance is greatly expected.
2.
The intimate combination of GQDs with semiconductors of different dimensionality (e.g., 0D, 1D, 2D, or 3D) in an appropriate fashion plays an important role for boosting efficient interfacial charge carriers transfer. Generally, GQDs are physical contacted with other materials by mixing routes in previous works, which normally gives rise to unfavorable interfacial interaction. Effectively forming chemical bond between these two components can reinforce the interfacial integration, which is beneficial for improving the photogenerated charge carriers transfer efficiency. Moreover, GQDs-based nanocomposites should be more widely exploited in some particular fields such as solar energy conversion and environmental issues, for example, CO2 photoreduction, water purification, photocatalytic nitrogen fixation etc. We strongly believe that more exciting research progress on GQDs-based systems for highly efficient photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis will be witnessed.
3.
It is advisable for scientist communities to develop LbL assembly techniques apart from conventional synthetic method to fabricate diverse GQDs-based multifunctional thin films or hierarchical nanostructures with tunable physicochemical properties [110]. The intrinsic negatively charged surface of GQDs makes it a suitable building block for LbL assembly of various nano-architectures. It is anticipated that our current review article will provide enriched information on the photocatalytic and PEC properties of GQDs-based nanomaterials for advanced solar energy conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The award program for Minjiang Professorship from Fuzhou University is greatly acknowledged. The financial support from the project of Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (21703038) is acknowledged. This research was supported by the Nanyang Technological University Start-up Grant (M4081326).
